claims.claim_text: A platform engineer at a payments company is paged during a promotion because checkout latency has tripled, while service, database, and network dashboards look normal.
claims.anchor_text: A platform engineer at a payments company gets paged because checkout latency has tripled during a promotion. The application team insists their service metrics look normal, the database team sees no sustained CPU pressure, and the networking team cannot reproduce the issue from their test pod. Everyone has a dashboard, but each dashboard shows only the part of the system its owner expected to instrument.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a checkout system, a platform engineer may be paged because latency has climbed while each team’s familiar dashboard still looks normal. The application team may see normal service metrics, the database team may see no sustained CPU pressure, and the networking team may not reproduce the symptom from a test pod. Everyone has a dashboard, but each dashboard shows only the part of the system its owner expected to instrument.
claims.rationale: This is framed like a concrete incident with a precise impact claim but has no attribution, so the rewrite removes the false specificity while preserving the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: Pixie assumes Linux Kubernetes nodes with eBPF support and privileged PEM pods.
claims.rationale: Pixie's requirements document states Linux node support, kernel requirements, and privileged access requirements for `vizier-pem-*` pods.

claims.claim_text: Pixie uses eBPF to automatically collect Kubernetes application telemetry without manual instrumentation.
claims.rationale: The Pixie README directly describes eBPF-based auto-telemetry and scriptability.

claims.claim_text: eBPF programs are verified before loading and run under constrained access rules enforced by the Linux verifier.
claims.rationale: The Linux BPF verifier documentation describes verifier safety checks, pointer types, bounds checks, and function-call constraints.

claims.claim_text: Pixie stores telemetry locally in cluster memory with short retention, so it is not a long-term observability store by itself.
claims.rationale: Pixie's FAQ states that telemetry is stored in memory on nodes, retention is generally on the order of hours, and long-term retention requires integrations.

claims.claim_text: The Pixie Edge Module is deployed to each node via a DaemonSet and acts as Pixie's per-node data collector and short-term store.
claims.rationale: The Pixie architecture reference identifies PEMs as data collectors deployed to each node via a DaemonSet and short-term in-memory stores.

claims.claim_text: DaemonSets are the Kubernetes workload mechanism used to run a copy of a pod on selected or all nodes.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes DaemonSet documentation directly explains the one-pod-per-node scheduling model.

claims.claim_text: Vizier is Pixie's in-cluster data plane and query path, coordinating script execution across PEMs.
claims.rationale: The architecture reference describes Vizier as Pixie's data plane and documents query broker script execution across PEMs.

claims.claim_text: Pixie metadata includes Kubernetes objects such as pods, services, namespaces, nodes, containers, and clusters.
claims.rationale: The Pixie FAQ lists captured Kubernetes metadata types, including cluster, namespace, node, pod, service, and container metadata.

claims.claim_text: Pixie uses NATS as an internal message bus for Vizier components.
claims.rationale: The architecture reference names NATS as Pixie's message bus system for messages between pods and services within Vizier.

claims.claim_text: Pixie supports hosted Pixie Cloud and self-hosted deployments, with a cloud connector path between Vizier and Pixie Cloud.
claims.rationale: Pixie's FAQ states that hosted and self-hosted Pixie exist and explains the Pixie Cloud control path and metadata handling.

claims.claim_text: Pixie CLI and Helm workflows support compatibility checking, deployment, and status verification such as `px deploy --check_only`, `px get viziers`, and `px get pems`.
claims.rationale: The Helm install guide documents requirement checks and verification commands including `px get viziers` and `px get pems`.

claims.claim_text: Pixie supports deploying to a subset of nodes, configuring custom cluster names, memory usage, data access mode, metadata storage, registries, and PEM flags.
claims.span_hint: Deployment and Validation section; Operating Pixie Responsibly
claims.rationale: Pixie's deploy-options reference lists these deployment controls and shows CLI and Helm examples.

claims.claim_text: PxL is Pixie's Python-like/DataFrame-oriented language for querying Pixie telemetry through UI, CLI, and API.
claims.rationale: The PxL reference describes PxL as a Python dialect influenced by Pandas and using DataFrames; the Using Pixie docs state that UI, CLI, and API execute PxL scripts.

claims.claim_text: Pixie exposes built-in data tables such as `http_events`, `dns_events`, `pgsql_events`, `mysql_events`, `conn_stats`, `process_stats`, and `network_stats`.
claims.rationale: Pixie's data table documentation lists these tables and their descriptions.

claims.claim_text: Pixie automatically traces protocols including HTTP, DNS, MySQL, PostgreSQL, NATS, and other supported protocols, with support depending on protocol and encryption-library constraints.
claims.rationale: The Pixie data-sources page lists automatically traced protocols and supported encryption libraries.

claims.claim_text: Pixie can expose full-body protocol messages and sensitive payload-like fields unless restricted by access mode or operator policy.
claims.span_hint: lines 519 and 658; Did You Know and Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: Pixie's deploy-options docs define `Full` and `Restricted` data access modes and state that restricted mode redacts potentially sensitive request/response bodies and headers.

claims.claim_text: Pixie can export transformed DataFrame results in OpenTelemetry format to an OpenTelemetry collector.
claims.rationale: The Pixie OpenTelemetry integration tutorial shows `px.export` with `px.otel.Data` and an OpenTelemetry collector endpoint; the shared pool covers the Collector role.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus is used for durable numeric metrics and alerting, and OpenTelemetry is used for application-owned traces and semantic spans.
claims.rationale: The shared pool's Prometheus overview supports metrics and alerting, while the OpenTelemetry traces docs support spans, attributes, and semantic tracing concepts.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes NodeLocal DNSCache is an available cluster DNS improvement option.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes NodeLocal DNSCache documentation directly supports the named mitigation option.

claims.claim_text: The hands-on exercise applies a manifest from the Pixie demo repository.
claims.rationale: The Pixie demos repository contains the `simple-gotracing` demo referenced by the exercise manifest URL.

further_reading.title: Pixie GitHub README
further_reading.why_relevant: Best allowlisted overview of Pixie's eBPF-based auto-telemetry, local query model, and major use cases.

further_reading.title: Pixie Architecture Reference
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents PEM, Vizier, Metadata, NATS, Cloud Connector, and the in-cluster architecture used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Collector
further_reading.why_relevant: Supports the module's recommendation to convert Pixie discoveries into durable exported telemetry.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes DaemonSet
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the node-coverage model behind Pixie PEM validation.

notes: I skipped diagrams, code blocks, quiz prompts, and exercise instructions unless nearby prose made a concrete vendor capability, security, or standards claim. Pixie documentation is mirrored in GitHub/raw.githubusercontent.com, so most Pixie-specific claims can be supported without allowlist expansion; the opening incident-style paragraph still needs a deterministic rewrite because its precise scenario is unattributed.
